what_it_does: Acceptance test that exercises the generation-policy repetition-penalty ladder wired into cognition.run_role and the transport passthrough for repetition_penalty, using monkeypatched fake client.complete and urllib.request.urlopen to record calls and request bodies.
observations: Monkeypatches cognition.client.complete with a factory-generated fake that records repetition_penalty, temperature, and meta usage while driving finish_reason from a sequence.
Monkeypatches urllib.request.urlopen to capture the JSON request body sent by the real openai_transport.
Relies on a seeded registry policy named capture_default whose rep_penalty_ladder is [1.1, 1.2].
Implements a hand-rolled check() helper that increments a global PASS counter and sets an ok flag.
Tests cover policy=None byte-identical path, policy-driven default rung, length-triggered escalation, ladder-exhaustion degenerate-loop error, and unknown-policy error.
Also verifies that the transport now forwards repetition_penalty into the request body and omits it when not supplied.
standouts: Behavioral verification of the real transport request bytes rather than source-string inspection.
A single acceptance file combines ladder-logic tests with end-to-end transport passthrough verification.
Explicit fail-loud contract tests for both ladder exhaustion and unknown policy ids.
conventions: Uses sys.path insertion and os.chdir to run from the repo root.
Restores monkeypatched globals in finally blocks.
Docstring enumerates numbered claims (O2.1–O2.5) that the code then checks.
Uses a Pydantic BaseModel as a trivial output schema for the test role.
concerns: Global mutable state (PASS, ok, _orig_complete, _real_uo) and monkeypatching of shared modules create cross-test interference risk if not run in isolation.
The transport test uses a lambda with a side-effect tuple-indexing expression, which is terse but hard to read.
The test is tightly coupled to the seeded policy capture_default and assumes a two-rung ladder.
No use of a standard test runner or fixture framework.
notes: A behavioral acceptance test that guards the generation-policy ladder contract and the repetition_penalty transport passthrough by faking the client and the HTTP layer.
questions: Where is generation_policy_registry and its policy_for method implemented?
What is the exact signature and behavior of cognition.run_role and the meta out-parameter?
Where is the capture_default policy file discovered by the registry?
How does the real transport populate finish_reason in meta?
Is this test intended to run in CI alongside unit tests or as a standalone acceptance script?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose in the docstring matches the file: it verifies the ladder is read from the registry, escalates on length, fails loud on exhaustion and unknown policy, and confirms the transport passthrough.
intention_signals: The file is trying to prove that run_role dynamically applies repetition-penalty rungs from file-discovered generation policies, escalates when the model hits a length finish, and that the configured value actually reaches the model through the transport layer.
novelty: Merges fake-client ladder-logic acceptance with a live-transport byte-capture test in one script, and uses a meta out-parameter simulation to drive finish_reason without a real model.
contribution: A runnable acceptance test that documents and enforces the generation-policy ladder contract: default-path unchanged, registry-sourced rungs, length escalation, degenerate-loop exhaustion error, unknown-policy error, and transport passthrough of repetition_penalty.
summary_for_embedding: An acceptance test that verifies the generation-policy repetition-penalty ladder in run_role and the transport passthrough by monkeypatching the client and HTTP layer to record calls and request bodies.
intention_for_embedding: To prove the ladder logic is wired into run_role from the registry, escalates on length, fails loud on exhaustion or unknown policy, and that repetition_penalty reaches the model request body.

symbols.does: Test assertion helper that prints ok/FAIL and updates global PASS and ok state.
symbols.description: A lightweight assertion function used throughout the script; it increments PASS on success and sets the global ok flag to False on failure, printing a labeled result line.

symbols.does: Trivial Pydantic output schema with a single text field defaulting to 'ok'.
symbols.description: Defines the output shape used by the test role, matching the schema-based call pattern that run_role and run_swarm expect.

symbols.does: Builds a fake cognition.client.complete that records options and drives finish_reason from a sequence.
symbols.description: Returns a _fake function preconfigured with a finish_reason sequence; the fake records repetition_penalty, temperature, and whether a meta dict was provided, then returns a schema instance.

symbols.does: The generated fake complete() that writes finish_reason into meta and records per-call options.
symbols.description: Impersonates the real transport-level complete signature, uses the next entry from finish_seq as the simulated finish_reason, stores it in the meta out-param, and appends a dict of observed options to the calls list.

symbols.does: Minimal fake HTTP response object for urllib.request.urlopen.
symbols.description: A tiny context-manager-compatible response class that returns the provided bytes from read() so the transport test can avoid real network I/O.
Initializes the fake response with the byte string that read() will later return.
Implements the read method expected by urlopen callers, returning the JSON payload provided at construction.

symbols.does: Returns self for use as a context manager.
symbols.description: Supports the with-statement protocol used by urlopen responses.

symbols.does: Exits the context manager without suppressing exceptions.
symbols.description: Returns False so any exception propagates normally after the with block.

symbols.does: Fake urlopen that captures the deserialized request body and returns a fake OpenAI-style response.
symbols.description: Intercepts urllib.request.urlopen during the transport test, stores the JSON request body in a shared dict, and returns a _FakeR containing a minimal chat-completion JSON response.
